bidirectional_dilemma.context: If an individual consumes a significant amount of water, they will experience a state of hydration. Conversely, if excessive amounts of sugar are ingested, a sugar crash will ensue. It is known that at least one of the following statements is true: either the Jane consumes ample water or she will not experience a sugar crash. However, the actual veracity of either statement remains ambiguous, as it could be the case that only the first statement is true, only the second statement is true, or bot
bidirectional_dilemma.question: Can we say at least one of the following must always be true? (a) she will feel hydrated and (b) she doesn't eat too much sugar

<think>
We are asked to decide whether at least one of the statements about Tom's academic performance or viewing habits must be true. The context supplies two conditional relationships and a disjunction, so we will examine how they interact to see which alternatives can be enforced.

This premise gives us the direct link between diligent study and good grades, which will be useful if we ever learn that Tom studies diligently. It sets up the rule that will let us derive (a) later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Tom studies diligently, then he will obtain good grades.</text>
<logic>good_grades(tom) :- diligent_study(tom)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the converse relationship: excessive TV watching blocks academic success. This will allow us to infer the negation of a TV‑watching habit from the presence of good grades.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Tom watches too much television, then he will not achieve good grades.</text>
<logic>~good_grades(tom) :- excessive_tv(tom)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides a concrete alternative: either Tom studies diligently or he excels academically. This is the only explicit fact about Tom's possible attributes, so we will need to see how it can combine with the other rules.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is given that either Tom studies diligently or he is an academic star.</text>
<logic>diligent_study(tom) v good_grades(tom)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Tom excels academically gives us concrete information that satisfies one side of the disjunction from PREMISE_3. Knowing this lets us resolve part of the ambiguity.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We have the fact that Tom is an academic star.</text>
<logic>good_grades(tom)</logic>
</step>

Because Tom is known to be an academic star, the conditional from PREMISE_2 tells us that if he watched too much TV, he could not be an academic star. Since we already have the opposite, we must conclude that Tom does not watch too much TV. This is an application of modus tollens.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Tom is an academic star, the rule that excessive TV would prevent academic success lets us infer that Tom does not watch too much television.</text>
<logic>~excessive_tv(tom)</logic>
</step>

We now have two statements: Tom is an academic star (from PREMISE_4) and the rule that diligent study leads to good grades (PREMISE_1). The contrapositive of that rule says that having good grades implies diligent study, so we can infer that Tom studies diligently. This follows by modus tollens again, turning the presence of good grades into the antecedent of the conditional.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Having established that Tom is an academic star, and knowing that diligent study guarantees good grades, we can deduce that Tom must be studying diligently.</text>
<logic>diligent_study(tom)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Tom studies diligently (STEP_6) and remembering that diligent study guarantees good grades (PREMISE_1), we can directly assert that Tom obtains good grades. This is a straightforward modus ponens move.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Tom's diligent study and the rule linking diligent study to good grades, we conclude that Tom indeed gets good grades.</text>
<logic>good_grades(tom)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since Tom now has good grades, the rule that excessive TV would block good grades tells us that Tom cannot watch too much television.</text>
<logic>~excessive_tv(tom)</logic>

<step n="9" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because Tom gets good grades, the disjunction that either diligent study or academic success holds is satisfied, allowing us to accept the alternative of good grades as a valid outcome.</text>
<logic>good_grades(tom) v diligent_study(tom)</logic>

<step n="10" type="derived" from="4,9">

Therefore, the statement that Tom has good grades is established, satisfying part (a).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">

<logic>good_grades(tom)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that Tom is not excessive tv (from PREMISE_5) and the rule that excessive tv would block good grades (PREMISE_2), we can infer the contrapositive: if he is not excessive tv, then the rule does not force the opposite, so we can assert that the negative literal of the disjunction is false. However, the earlier steps showed that Tom does have good grades, and the disjunction only required one side, so the alternative (not excessive tv) does not need to hold to make the disjunction true. constructive_dilemma.context: If someone is painting a picture, then they will frame it. Similarly, the natural course of action for a writer would be to publish their completed story. In this scenario, it is certain that at least one of the following statements holds true: (1) John is currently engrossed in painting a picture, or (2) John is currently immersed in the act of writing a story. It should be emphasized that we are unaware of which statement specifically applies, as there is a possibility that either (1) alone is
constructive_dilemma.question: Can we say at least one of the following must always be true? (a) he will frame it and (b) he will publish it.

completions.text:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether either of the statements "John will frame it" or "John will publish it" must be true, given the information about his current activity. To answer, we need to examine how the provided facts and rules interact with the disjunction.

The first rule tells us that any painting activity guarantees framing. This connection is important because if we can show that John is painting, we will automatically obtain the framing conclusion, which corresponds to statement (a).
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If John is painting, then he will frame it.</text>
<logic>framing(john) :- painting(john)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links the act of writing to publishing. Knowing this helps us see that establishing that John is writing would immediately give us the publishing conclusion, which corresponds to statement (b).
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If John is writing, then he will publish it.</text>
<logic>publishing(john) :- writing(john)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of the two activities—painting or writing—holds for John. This provides the starting point for evaluating the truth of the two target statements, since each activity leads to a separate conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either John is painting or John is writing (or both).</text>
<logic>painting(john) v writing(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3">
<text>Since we know John is either painting or writing, we can conclude that he is indeed painting (the other case would still satisfy the disjunction).</text>
<logic>painting(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Given that John is painting, the earlier rule tells us that he must frame it.</text>
<logic>framing(john)</logic>

We are asked to determine whether the conjunction of two specific actions—framing a picture and publishing a story—must hold for John given the information supplied. To answer, we must examine the disjunction that provides the only factual guarantees about what John is currently doing.

The first rule tells us that framing an artwork follows directly from painting it, linking the activity of painting to the subsequent act of framing. This conditional will be useful later if we ever establish that John is painting.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If someone is painting a picture, then they will frame it.</text>
<logic>frame(john) :- paint(john)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects the act of writing a story to its eventual publication, meaning that writing implies publishing. This rule will become relevant if we can show that John is writing.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone is writing a story, then they will publish it.</text>
<logic>publish(john) :- write(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either John is painting a picture or John is writing a story.</text>
<logic>paint(john) v write(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3">
<text>Since the disjunction tells us that John is either painting a picture or writing a story, the only way to satisfy that statement is to accept that John does not paint a picture, so the remaining alternative must be true, meaning John writes a story.</text>
<logic>write(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because we have established that John writes a story, and the rule says that any person who writes a story will publish it, we can conclude that John will publish it.</text>
<logic>publish(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>However, the rule about painting implies that if John painted a picture, then he would frame it. Although we derived that he writes a story, this rule does not affect the publishing conclusion, and it cannot be applied because we have no evidence that John paints.</text>
<logic>~frame(john)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,5">
<text>Similarly, the rule linking painting to framing would give us a way to infer framing, but again we lack the antecedent (painting), so no framing can be concluded. Consequently, neither framing nor publishing can be guaranteed simultaneously.</text>
<logic>~(frame(john) v publish(john))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7">
<text>Thus, we cannot assert that at least one of the propositions—framing a picture or publishing a story—must be true; in fact, both cannot be proved, making the query false.</text>
<logic>~(frame(john) v publish(john))</logic>
</step>

Because we have established that John writes a story, and the rule says that any person who writes a story will publish it, we can conclude that John will publish it.

However, the rule about painting implies that if John painted a picture, then he would frame it. Although we derived that he writes a story, this rule does not affect the publishing conclusion, and it cannot be applied because we have no evidence that John paints.

Similarly, the rule linking painting to framing would give us a way to infer framing, but again we lack the antecedent (painting), so no framing can be concluded. Consequently, neither framing nor publishing can be guaranteed simultaneously.

On the other hand, if they are diligently preparing for their upcoming exams, they usually choose to dedicate their time to studying at the library. constructive_dilemma.question: Can we say at least one of the following must always be true? (a) she will go to the movies. and (b) she will go to the library.

It is uncertain which statement is true, as it could be either one or both. Linda may not conserve electricity or incur a high electricity bill.

Our task is to determine whether either "Linda does not turn off the lights" or "Linda does not use a generator" must hold, i.e., whether the disjunction (¬turn_off_lights ∨ ¬use_generator) is always true.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Linda turns off the lights, then she will save electricity.</text>
<logic>save_electricity(linda) :- turn_off_lights(linda)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Linda uses a generator, then she will incur a high electricity bill.</text>
<logic>high_bill(linda) :- use_generator(linda)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Linda does not save electricity or she does not pay a high bill.</text>
<logic>~save_electricity(linda) v ~high_bill(linda)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3">
<text>From the disjunction and the fact that Linda saves electricity, we can infer that she does not pay a high bill.</text>
<logic>~high_bill(linda)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Because Linda does not pay a high bill and using a generator would imply she does, we infer that she does not use a generator.</text>
<logic>~use_generator(linda)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since Linda does not use a generator, the antecedent of the first rule cannot be true, so we infer that she does not turn off the lights.</text>
<logic>~turn_off_lights(linda)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived" from="5,6">
<text>Combining both derived facts, we conclude that Linda does not turn off the lights or she does not use a generator, which directly satisfies the original disjunction.</text>
<logic>~turn_off_lights(linda) v ~use_generator(linda)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Linda is not use generator, so the conclusion is derived.
Answer: Yes
</think>

Answer: Yes

</step>

The first rule tells us that turning off the lights guarantees saving electricity. This relationship will be useful later when we consider the disjunction involving saving electricity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Linda turns off the lights, then she saves electricity.</text>
<logic>saving_electricity(linda) :- turning_off_lights(linda)</logic>

The second rule connects using a generator to incurring a high electricity bill. Knowing this helps us link the generator usage to the bill disjunction later.

</step>

The disjunction states that either Linda does not save electricity or she does not pay a high bill. This provides a direct conflict with the implications of the previous two rules if both were true.

<logic>~saving_electricity(linda) v ~high_bill(linda)</logic>
</step>

<text>We are given that Linda turns off the lights.</text>
<logic>turning_off_lights(linda)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that Linda turned off the lights and the first rule, we can apply modus ponens: the antecedent (turning off the lights) holds, so the consequent (saving electricity) must also hold. Hence we can conclude that Linda saves electricity.

Therefore, we infer that Linda does not pay a high bill.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">

disjunctive_syllogism.context: It is known that one of the following options is true: someone goes to a museum or someone visits a park. The specific scenario could involve only the option to go to a museum being true, or only the option to visit a park being true, or both options being true. However, it is stated that Jill is unable to go to a museum.
disjunctive_syllogism.question: Does this imply that she can visit a park?

completions.text:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Jill can visit a park given two pieces of information: a disjunction that either she goes to a museum or she visits a park, and the explicit fact that she does not go to a museum. Our goal is to determine the truth of the claim that she visits a park.

The first premise gives us a logical choice: at least one of the two activities must hold. Recognizing this helps us see that the disjunction sets up a situation where the truth of one alternative guarantees the falsity of the other, provided the other is known not to be true.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The statement that either someone goes to a museum or someone visits a park.</text>
<logic>goes_to_museum(jill) v visits_park(jill)</logic>
</step>

The second premise supplies a concrete fact that Jill is excluded from the museum activity. This negative information will be crucial for eliminating the museum option from the disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The statement that Jill is not able to go to a museum.</text>
<logic>~goes_to_museum(jill)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Jill cannot go to a museum and the disjunction tells us that she must either go to a museum or visit a park, the only way for the disjunction to stay true is for the remaining alternative—visiting a park—to be true. This is a classic application of disjunctive syllogism, where removing a false disjunct forces the other to be true.
<step n="3" type="derived" from="2,1">
<text>From Jill not going to a museum and the knowledge that either she goes to a museum or visits a park, we infer that Jill must visit a park.</text>
<logic>visits_park(jill)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Jill is visits park, so the conclusion is Yes.
Answer: Yes
</think>

Answer: Yes

steps.text: The statement that either someone goes to a museum or someone visits a park.
The statement that Jill is not able to go to a museum.
From Jill not going to a museum and the knowledge that either she goes to a museum or visits a park, we infer that Jill must visit a park.

completions.text:

</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Once lily starts the project, she will finish it on time.</text>
<logic>finished_project(lily) :- starting_project(lily)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Lily bought all the necessary supplies is the concrete starting point for the chain of reasoning. This factual premise activates the first conditional rule.

<text>Lily bought all the necessary supplies.</text>
<logic>supplies_bought(lily)</logic>
</step>

By combining the fact that Lily bought supplies with the rule that supply purchase leads to project initiation, we can directly infer that Lily has started the project. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Therefore, lily has started the project.</text>
<logic>starting_project(lily)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Lily's project has been started, we can apply the second rule, which states that any project initiation results in completion on time. Hence, Lily will finish the project as scheduled.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Thus, lily will finish the project on time.</text>
<logic>finished_project(lily)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Lily is finished project, so the conclusion is Lily is finished project, so the answer is Yes.
Answer: Yes
</think>

<logic>admiration(adam) :- cleaning_room(adam)</logic>

The second premise tells us that parental admiration guarantees a recognition or a reward. It creates the bridge from admiration to the desired conclusion about a reward, so we need to establish admiration first.

completions.text:

The rule tells us that visiting the park guarantees wearing a mask, which means that anyone who is in the park must have a mask on. This connection will be important for evaluating whether the absence of a mask can prevent park attendance.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever John decides to go to the park, he must wear a mask.</text>
<logic>wearing_mask(john) :- visiting_park(john)</logic>
</step>

The statement that John does not wear a mask directly contradicts the condition required by the rule, because the rule says park visitors must have masks. Knowing this fact lets us test the rule in reverse.

<logic>~wearing_mask(john)</logic>
</step>

Given that the rule links park visitation to mask wearing, and we know that park visitors must have masks, we can apply modus tollens: if someone lacks a mask, they cannot satisfy the antecedent of the rule, so they cannot be visiting the park. Therefore, from the fact that John has no mask we infer that John is not visiting the park.

Therefore John is not visiting park, so the conclusion is Yes.

modus_tollens.context: If a car experiences a flat tyre, it is necessary to have it towed. Unfortunately, Jack was not aware of the car's tow-worthy condition.
modus_tollens.question: Does this imply that Jack didn't notice the car has a flat tyre?

completions.text:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the lack of awareness about the car's tow status leads to the conclusion that Jack did not notice a flat tyre. The goal is to see if the given facts and the conditional rule allow us to infer a negative observation about the tyre.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a car has a flat tyre, then it must be towed.</text>
<logic>towed(jack) :- flat_tyre(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Jack was not aware that the car is tow-worthy.</text>
<logic>~tow_aware(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="derived" from="2">
<text>Because Jack was not aware of the car's tow condition, we can infer that he could not possibly have noticed a flat tyre.</text>
<logic>~flat_tyre(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>Since the car does not have a flat tyre, the rule about flat tyres leading to being towed does not apply, so we cannot infer anything about Jack's observation.</text>
<logic>~observed(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="derived" from="2,1">
<text>Because Jack was not aware of the car's tow condition, the fact that a flat tyre would make it towable does not force the car to be towed, leaving his status as a flat tyre undecided.</text>
<logic>~flat_tyre(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5">
<text>Therefore, Jack indeed did not observe a flat tyre.</text>
<logic>~observed(jack)</logic>
</step>

Answer: Yes
</think>

Answer: No

modus_tollens.context: If an individual possesses a car, they have the capability to embark on an extended expedition by road. However, in this particular scenario, Judy lacks the ability to embark on an extended expedition by road.
modus_tollens.question: Does this imply that Judy doesn't have a car?

completions.text:

We are asked to determine whether the lack of an extended road expedition for Judy necessarily means she does not own a car. To answer this, we must examine the conditional relationship between owning a car and being able to go on such an expedition.

The conditional statement links car ownership to the expedition ability, which is crucial for applying modus tollens later. Recognizing this implication lets us see how a false conclusion about the expedition can affect the truth of the car ownership claim.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if someone has a car, then they can go on an extended road expedition.</text>
<logic>road_expedition(judy) :- has_car(judy)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Judy cannot embark on an extended road expedition gives us a concrete instance where the consequent of the conditional is false. This situation activates the conditions needed for modus tollens.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Judy cannot go on an extended road expedition.</text>
<logic>~road_expedition(judy)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus tollens, we take the false consequent (~road_expedition) and the original conditional (has_car → road_expedition) to infer the negation of the antecedent, ~has_car. This deduction follows because if the consequence were true, the antecedent could not be false.
<step n="3" type="derived" from="2,1">
<text>Because Judy cannot go on an extended road expedition and the rule tells us that having a car would make it possible, we infer that Judy does not have a car.</text>
<logic>~has_car(judy)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Judy is not has car, so the conclusion is Yes.
Answer: Yes
</think>

Answer: Yes
